O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK ASSESSMENT — PERIOD ENDING 2026-05-28

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — BREACH | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

The supply chain risk profile is the most acute operational exposure this period. Three of the four O-01 KRIs are in breach, and an escalation trigger has been activated for Quanta [SUP005].

Single-source concentration stands at 62.6% of $12,610M total spend — a material breach of the 50% threshold. The three single-source relationships (TSMC, Foxconn, Quanta) account for approximately $7,900M of spend and represent irreplaceable near-term production capacity. TSMC alone contributes $4,200M for CPU semiconductors with an 18-week lead time; any disruption here has no credible short-term substitute path. The stress model confirms revenue at risk of $9.1B and a 5-month recovery horizon if a major disruption event occurs — a figure that dwarfs the estimated $382M saving from dual-sourcing and $305M from inventory buffering, both of which remain unrealised.

Minimum inventory cover has breached the 4-week threshold at 3.9 weeks. This figure is set by the lowest-cover single-source supplier, which is Quanta [SUP005] at exactly 3.9 weeks. Quanta's position is the most concerning in the portfolio: financial health score of 61 (below the 65 distress threshold), on-time delivery of only 88.7% (the weakest of the three single-source suppliers), and a rejection rate of 1.2% — all simultaneously. The dual-condition escalation trigger (financial_health <65 AND inventory <4wk for a single-source supplier) is met. If Quanta experiences further financial deterioration, a draw-down of even one week's cover would leave the ODM-Laptops line with no buffer. The 6-week lead time on Quanta product means a recovery window of at least 6 weeks minimum, during which laptop product revenue would be materially impaired.

Yangtze Memory [SUP008], while multi-sourced, carries a financial health score of 55 — the weakest in the supplier portfolio. Combined with the 2-count distress flag (amber threshold: 1), the supplier financial risk is worsening. The geographic concentration data adds a macro overlay: 53.5% of spend is domiciled in Taiwan and 21.8% in China. A Taiwan Strait stress event or cross-strait trade restriction would simultaneously impair TSMC ($4,200M), Quanta ($1,600M), and potentially components from other Taiwan-based suppliers — concentrating the 95th-percentile supply chain VaR of $2,634M into a single geopolitical scenario.

BCM assessment: The organisation cannot sustain full production beyond approximately 3.9 weeks without Quanta, and cannot replace TSMC CPU supply within any operationally meaningful timeframe given the 18-week lead time. The 5-month recovery model from the stress output should be treated as a best-case scenario assuming immediate dual-sourcing action is initiated. EBITDA exposure includes premium spot-market sourcing costs, potential line stoppages, and contractual penalties — none of which are quantified in the data provided but which would compound the $2,634M VaR figure.

O-02 CYBER AND IT — BREACH | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

The cyber risk domain shows the highest concentration of simultaneous KRI breaches (five breaches, four ambers) and the control environment is assessed as materially degraded.

The escalation trigger is met: supplier_cyber_resilience_assess_pct is 0% — meaning zero third-party suppliers have completed a cyber resilience assessment — while security_incidents_mtd stands at 4. This combination (zero assessment coverage, active incident volume) means the organisation has no visibility into the cyber posture of any supplier, including the three single-source suppliers handling ~$7,900M of spend, during a period of confirmed security activity.

MTTD is 11 days, breaching both the 10-day breach threshold and approaching the 14-day escalation trigger. This means that threats active in the environment are, on average, undetected for 11 days. Mean time to respond is 18 days, indicating that even once detected, remediation is slow. The combined MTTD + MTTR window of 29 days represents the period during which a threat actor can operate in the environment before being contained. The 8 critical vulnerabilities open for more than 30 days (amber: 5, breach: 10 — currently amber but proximate to breach) and 23 high vulnerabilities in the same state reinforce this exposure window.

Patch compliance at 71% is a breach of the 80% threshold, meaning nearly 3 in 10 systems are running unpatched. Privileged access has gone unreviewed for 45 days — no threshold is provided in the data, but 45 days of unreviewed privileged access during a period with 4 active security incidents represents a material lateral movement and privilege escalation risk. MFA coverage at 87% (amber) leaves 13% of accounts without multi-factor authentication, a known primary attack vector.

The OMS RTO is exactly at the breach threshold of 6 hours. Order management system downtime beyond 6 hours would directly impair revenue recognition and fulfilment capability. Given that supply chain continuity is already under stress (O-01), a cyber-induced OMS outage during a supply disruption event would create a compounding BCM failure.

Only 62% of third-party vendors have been assessed for cyber risk, and as noted, 0% of suppliers specifically have completed resilience assessments. The 38% of unassessed vendors represent an unknown but material blind spot.

BCM/EBITDA: An OMS outage exceeding the 6-hour RTO would directly interrupt order flow and revenue recognition. A ransomware or destructive attack — made more likely by the 11-day MTTD window — could extend that outage materially. The cost of incident response, potential regulatory notification obligations, and reputational impact are not quantified in the data but represent a significant EBITDA tail risk given the volume of active incidents.

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — AMBER | NO ESCALATION

Product quality is the most contained of the four operational risk domains this period. No safety incidents have been recorded (zero tolerance threshold: clear), field failure rate at 0.019% is well within the 0.05% amber threshold, and first-pass yield of 97.2% is healthy. These are genuine positives.

However, three amber signals warrant close monitoring. The supplier quality rejection rate of 0.68% has breached the 0.5% amber threshold. When disaggregated by the single-source supplier data, Quanta [SUP005] is the primary driver with a 1.2% rejection rate — the highest of the named suppliers and directly correlated with Quanta's broader distress profile. If Quanta's financial health continues to deteriorate, quality inputs to the ODM-Laptops line are likely to worsen further, driving warranty claims higher and potentially pushing the field failure rate toward the amber boundary.

Warranty claims of 1,842 month-to-date are noted; no threshold is provided in the data, but the absolute volume is a cost line that will rise if supplier quality degrades. The recall readiness score of 72 sits 8 points below the 80 amber threshold and only 2 points above the 70 breach threshold. The last recall simulation was run 8 months ago — a gap that is particularly concerning given that the recall readiness score is already amber. If a product safety event were to emerge (currently zero, but the near-miss event YTD count of 1 indicates the pathway exists), the organisation's ability to execute a rapid, coordinated recall is not fully assured.

EBITDA: The current warranty cost run rate and the direction of the supplier rejection rate suggest modest but growing pressure on warranty reserves. A deterioration in Quanta's quality output — plausible given the financial distress signal — would amplify this. A recall event, while not currently indicated by safety data, would represent a step-change EBITDA impact; the below-threshold recall readiness score means the cost and reputational impact of any future recall would be higher than it would be with a fully prepared function.

O-04 TALENT — BREACH | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

The talent domain has deteriorated to an escalation-level condition. Three flight-risk employees have been flagged in R&D — meeting the escalation trigger for three or more flight-risk employees in the same critical function. R&D is the innovation and product differentiation engine; concentrated departures here pose a direct threat to the product roadmap and to intellectual property continuity.

Critical open roles exceeding 60 days stands at 7 — breaching the threshold of 6. In Engineering specifically, the average time to fill a critical role is 68 days, against a compensation gap vs. market of 8.2%. The counter-offer acceptance rate in Engineering is 42%, indicating that when the organisation attempts to retain departing talent, it fails to do so in 58% of cases — a direct consequence of the 8.2% compensation gap. Engineering attrition at 14.2% annualised (amber, threshold 18% breach) is elevated and directionally worsening given the compensation gap and engagement signals.

IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% (amber: 12%) is approaching the amber threshold. This is directly relevant to the O-02 cyber posture: an understaffed or high-turnover IT-Operations function is consistent with the observed 71% patch compliance, 45-day unreviewed privileged access, and 11-day MTTD. The cyber control weaknesses in O-02 should not be read in isolation from IT-Operations capacity constraints.

SVP succession plan coverage at 72% is in amber territory (threshold: 80%), meaning more than one in four SVP-level roles has no identified successor. Given the R&D flight risk concentration, if any of those three flagged R&D employees hold senior leadership positions, the succession gap is compounding.

Employee engagement at 67% and internal promotion rate at 18% are indicators of a workforce that does not perceive clear career progression. These are structural drivers of the attrition observed in Engineering and the flight risk in R&D, not episodic events. Mandatory training completion at 91% is the single positive signal in this domain.

BCM/EBITDA: Talent risk translates into EBITDA through three channels visible in the data: (1) cost of unfilled roles and overtime/contractor spend to cover 7 open critical positions; (2) cost of failed retention — counter-offers accepted in only 42% of cases, meaning the organisation is incurring recruitment costs repeatedly; (3) the indirect cost of degraded cyber controls (O-02) attributable in part to IT-Operations capacity, and degraded product development velocity from R&D attrition. The 5-month supply chain recovery window (O-01) requires significant Engineering and procurement resource; the current talent constraint materially limits the organisation's ability to execute that recovery.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

O-03.override_justification: Expert override applied 2026-06-07 (EM-08 panel finding). Arithmetic rating: low (l=2, i=5). Override to: high (l=3, i=5). Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

Forward-looking: 3 senior R&D engineers under active hyperscaler recruitment (confirmed approaches from two major cloud providers); no documented SVP successors for 2 roles; compensation gap widening vs. hyperscaler benchmarks. Override reflects leading indicators not captured in lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off: 2026-06-07 (reissued EM-06 panel correction — prior text erroneously referenced 'senior traders'; corrected to R&D engineering population).

Single-source concentration stands at 62.6% of $12,610M total spend — a material breach of the 50% threshold. The three single-source relationships (TSMC, Foxconn, Quanta) account for approximately $7,900M of spend and represent irreplaceable near-term production capacity. TSMC alone contributes $4,200M for CPU semiconductors with an 18-week lead time; any disruption here has no credible short-term substitute path. The stress model confirms revenue at risk of $9.1B and a 5-month recovery horizon if a major disruption event occurs — a figure that dwarfs the estimated $382M saving from dual-sourcing and $305M from inventory buffering, both of which remain unrealised.

Minimum inventory cover has breached the 4-week threshold at 3.9 weeks. This figure is set by the lowest-cover single-source supplier, which is Quanta [SUP005] at exactly 3.9 weeks. Quanta's position is the most concerning in the portfolio: financial health score of 61 (below the 65 distress threshold), on-time delivery of only 88.7% (the weakest of the three single-source suppliers), and a rejection rate of 1.2% — all simultaneously. The dual-condition escalation trigger (financial_health <65 AND inventory <4wk for a single-source supplier) is met. If Quanta experiences further financial deterioration, a draw-down of even one week's cover would leave the ODM-Laptops line with no buffer. The 6-week lead time on Quanta product means a recovery window of at least 6 weeks minimum, during which laptop product revenue would be materially impaired.

Yangtze Memory [SUP008], while multi-sourced, carries a financial health score of 55 — the weakest in the supplier portfolio. Combined with the 2-count distress flag (amber threshold: 1), the supplier financial risk is worsening. The geographic concentration data adds a macro overlay: 53.5% of spend is domiciled in Taiwan and 21.8% in China. A Taiwan Strait stress event or cross-strait trade restriction would simultaneously impair TSMC ($4,200M), Quanta ($1,600M), and potentially components from other Taiwan-based suppliers — concentrating the 95th-percentile supply chain VaR of $2,634M into a single geopolitical scenario.

BCM assessment: The organisation cannot sustain full production beyond approximately 3.9 weeks without Quanta, and cannot replace TSMC CPU supply within any operationally meaningful timeframe given the 18-week lead time. The 5-month recovery model from the stress output should be treated as a best-case scenario assuming immediate dual-sourcing action is initiated. EBITDA exposure includes premium spot-market sourcing costs, potential line stoppages, and contractual penalties — none of which are quantified in the data provided but which would compound the $2,634M VaR figure.

O-02 CYBER AND IT — BREACH | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

The cyber risk domain shows the highest concentration of simultaneous KRI breaches (five breaches, four ambers) and the control environment is assessed as materially degraded.

The escalation trigger is met: supplier_cyber_resilience_assess_pct is 0% — meaning zero third-party suppliers have completed a cyber resilience assessment — while security_incidents_mtd stands at 4. This combination (zero assessment coverage, active incident volume) means the organisation has no visibility into the cyber posture of any supplier, including the three single-source suppliers handling ~$7,900M of spend, during a period of confirmed security activity.

MTTD is 11 days, breaching both the 10-day breach threshold and approaching the 14-day escalation trigger. This means that threats active in the environment are, on average, undetected for 11 days. Mean time to respond is 18 days, indicating that even once detected, remediation is slow. The combined MTTD + MTTR window of 29 days represents the period during which a threat actor can operate in the environment before being contained. The 8 critical vulnerabilities open for more than 30 days (amber: 5, breach: 10 — currently amber but proximate to breach) and 23 high vulnerabilities in the same state reinforce this exposure window.

Patch compliance at 71% is a breach of the 80% threshold, meaning nearly 3 in 10 systems are running unpatched. Privileged access has gone unreviewed for 45 days — no threshold is provided in the data, but 45 days of unreviewed privileged access during a period with 4 active security incidents represents a material lateral movement and privilege escalation risk. MFA coverage at 87% (amber) leaves 13% of accounts without multi-factor authentication, a known primary attack vector.

The OMS RTO is exactly at the breach threshold of 6 hours. Order management system downtime beyond 6 hours would directly impair revenue recognition and fulfilment capability. Given that supply chain continuity is already under stress (O-01), a cyber-induced OMS outage during a supply disruption event would create a compounding BCM failure.

Only 62% of third-party vendors have been assessed for cyber risk, and as noted, 0% of suppliers specifically have completed resilience assessments. The 38% of unassessed vendors represent an unknown but material blind spot.

BCM/EBITDA: An OMS outage exceeding the 6-hour RTO would directly interrupt order flow and revenue recognition. A ransomware or destructive attack — made more likely by the 11-day MTTD window — could extend that outage materially. The cost of incident response, potential regulatory notification obligations, and reputational impact are not quantified in the data but represent a significant EBITDA tail risk given the volume of active incidents.

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — AMBER | NO ESCALATION

Product quality is the most contained of the four operational risk domains this period. No safety incidents have been recorded (zero tolerance threshold: clear), field failure rate at 0.019% is well within the 0.05% amber threshold, and first-pass yield of 97.2% is healthy. These are genuine positives.

However, three amber signals warrant close monitoring. The supplier quality rejection rate of 0.68% has breached the 0.5% amber threshold. When disaggregated by the single-source supplier data, Quanta [SUP005] is the primary driver with a 1.2% rejection rate — the highest of the named suppliers and directly correlated with Quanta's broader distress profile. If Quanta's financial health continues to deteriorate, quality inputs to the ODM-Laptops line are likely to worsen further, driving warranty claims higher and potentially pushing the field failure rate toward the amber boundary.

Warranty claims of 1,842 month-to-date are noted; no threshold is provided in the data, but the absolute volume is a cost line that will rise if supplier quality degrades. The recall readiness score of 72 sits 8 points below the 80 amber threshold and only 2 points above the 70 breach threshold. The last recall simulation was run 8 months ago — a gap that is particularly concerning given that the recall readiness score is already amber. If a product safety event were to emerge (currently zero, but the near-miss event YTD count of 1 indicates the pathway exists), the organisation's ability to execute a rapid, coordinated recall is not fully assured.

EBITDA: The current warranty cost run rate and the direction of the supplier rejection rate suggest modest but growing pressure on warranty reserves. A deterioration in Quanta's quality output — plausible given the financial distress signal — would amplify this. A recall event, while not currently indicated by safety data, would represent a step-change EBITDA impact; the below-threshold recall readiness score means the cost and reputational impact of any future recall would be higher than it would be with a fully prepared function.

O-04 TALENT — BREACH | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

The talent domain has deteriorated to an escalation-level condition. Three flight-risk employees have been flagged in R&D — meeting the escalation trigger for three or more flight-risk employees in the same critical function. R&D is the innovation and product differentiation engine; concentrated departures here pose a direct threat to the product roadmap and to intellectual property continuity.

Critical open roles exceeding 60 days stands at 7 — breaching the threshold of 6. In Engineering specifically, the average time to fill a critical role is 68 days, against a compensation gap vs. market of 8.2%. The counter-offer acceptance rate in Engineering is 42%, indicating that when the organisation attempts to retain departing talent, it fails to do so in 58% of cases — a direct consequence of the 8.2% compensation gap. Engineering attrition at 14.2% annualised (amber, threshold 18% breach) is elevated and directionally worsening given the compensation gap and engagement signals.

IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% (amber: 12%) is approaching the amber threshold. This is directly relevant to the O-02 cyber posture: an understaffed or high-turnover IT-Operations function is consistent with the observed 71% patch compliance, 45-day unreviewed privileged access, and 11-day MTTD. The cyber control weaknesses in O-02 should not be read in isolation from IT-Operations capacity constraints.

SVP succession plan coverage at 72% is in amber territory (threshold: 80%), meaning more than one in four SVP-level roles has no identified successor. Given the R&D flight risk concentration, if any of those three flagged R&D employees hold senior leadership positions, the succession gap is compounding.

Employee engagement at 67% and internal promotion rate at 18% are indicators of a workforce that does not perceive clear career progression. These are structural drivers of the attrition observed in Engineering and the flight risk in R&D, not episodic events. Mandatory training completion at 91% is the single positive signal in this domain.

BCM/EBITDA: Talent risk translates into EBITDA through three channels visible in the data: (1) cost of unfilled roles and overtime/contractor spend to cover 7 open critical positions; (2) cost of failed retention — counter-offers accepted in only 42% of cases, meaning the organisation is incurring recruitment costs repeatedly; (3) the indirect cost of degraded cyber controls (O-02) attributable in part to IT-Operations capacity, and degraded product development velocity from R&D attrition. The 5-month supply chain recovery window (O-01) requires significant Engineering and procurement resource; the current talent constraint materially limits the organisation's ability to execute that recovery.

appetite_thresholds.domain_statement: Low appetite for supply chain disruption and product quality failures. Zero tolerance for major safety incidents. Low tolerance for cyber incidents: MTTD must stay below 5 days; patch compliance must exceed 95%.

# Strategic Risk Assessment — Current Cycle

---

## 1. DOMAIN STATUS
The strategic risk portfolio is in a **mixed-critical state**: S-01 has breached its KRI threshold on geopolitical concentration, S-02 is amber on competitive AI displacement, and S-03 is holding within acceptable bounds on M&A integration.

---

## 2. S-01 FINDING — GEOPOLITICAL RISK | **BREACH**
Five geopolitical signals are active this cycle, of which **four are high-severity**. The signals "US expands Entity List by 14 technology companies," "Taiwan Strait military exercises increase in frequency," and "Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM" are driving the high-severity count, alongside the EU reciprocal tariff review on Chinese electronics. The combination of accelerating US-China technology decoupling, elevated Taiwan Strait military activity, and regional trade retaliation represents a materially compressing operating environment for supply chain and market access.

---

## 3. S-02 FINDING — AI DISRUPTION RISK | **AMBER**
Three competitive AI signals are active: "Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM," "HP announces partnership with Google for Gemini-embedded Chromebook Pro," and "India announces PLI scheme expansion for domestic PC manufacturing." The first two signals indicate that direct hardware competitors are moving to embed on-device and cloud-integrated LLM capabilities into commercial product lines. The India PLI signal introduces an additional dimension of competitive manufacturing displacement at the regional level.

---

## 4. S-03 FINDING — M&A INTEGRATION RISK | **OK**
Three deals are active across varying stages: **CloudScale Systems** (ACQ001) is in Integration-Month 18 at USD 1,200M, **NeuralEdge AI** (ACQ002) is in due diligence at USD 450M, and **DataVault Security** (ACQ003) is in negotiation at USD 280M. Synergy delivery is tracking at **84.0% against target**, which supports the OK status. The pipeline carries USD 1,930M in combined deal value, with the two pre-close deals yet to generate integration exposure.

---

## 5. TOP ACTIONS

| Risk | Top Action |
|---|---|
| **S-01** | Convene an executive supply chain war-game specifically scoped to Entity List expansion and Taiwan Strait escalation scenarios to identify single-point dependencies requiring immediate mitigation. |
| **S-02** | Accelerate an internal product roadmap review to assess whether on-device AI capability gaps versus Dell and HP can be closed within the current development cycle or require a strategic partnership decision. |
| **S-03** | Conduct a focused synergy recovery analysis on CloudScale Systems (ACQ001, Month 18) to close the 16-point gap to full target before the integration window normalizes. |

---

## 6. ESCALATION
**YES** — S-01 KRI status is **BREACH**. Escalation to executive leadership and the board risk committee is required this cycle.

F-01.agent_findings:
## Financial Risk Domain Assessment — 2026-05-28

### F-01: FX & Commodity Exposure

**Status: AMBER | Trend: DETERIORATING**

The FX-only portfolio (per CF-04 2026-06-07 scope definition) carries an aggregate unhedged exposure of USD 4,080M against an amber threshold of USD 4,000M and a breach threshold of USD 5,000M. The portfolio average hedge ratio of 46.3% sits within the amber band (45%–60%). No single FX position meets the 60% minimum hedge ratio target.

Position-level detail:
- **FX001 USD_CNY (Revenue, JPMorgan):** Gross USD 3,200M, hedged USD 1,400M (43.8%). Hedge ratio is below the 45% breach floor. Unhedged exposure USD 1,800M — the largest single unhedged FX position in the portfolio. Unrealised P&L: USD -8.4M. Maturity: 6 months.
- **FX002 USD_EUR (Revenue, Deutsche Bank):** Gross USD 2,800M, hedged USD 1,200M (42.9%). Hedge ratio is also below the 45% breach floor. Unhedged exposure USD 1,600M. Unrealised P&L: +USD 7.2M (only profitable FX position). Maturity: 12 months.
- **FX003 USD_JPY (Cost, HSBC):** Gross USD 980M, hedged USD 600M (61.2%). Only position above the 60% amber threshold. Unrealised P&L: USD -15.6M — largest FX loss position. Maturity: 3 months, presenting near-term roll risk.
- **FX004 USD_KRW (Cost, Citibank):** Gross USD 620M, hedged USD 320M (51.6%). Within amber band. Unrealised P&L: USD -9.8M. Maturity: 6 months.

FX-only unrealised P&L aggregate: USD -47.0M (flowing through operating income to EBITDA).

Commodity positions (supplementary context, outside F-01 KRI scope): COM001 DRAM (Goldman Sachs, USD 890M gross, USD 400M hedged, 44.9%, unrealised USD -12.3M, 3-month maturity) and COM002 NAND (JPMorgan, USD 650M gross, USD 280M hedged, 43.1%, unrealised USD -8.1M, 3-month maturity). Combined commodity unrealised P&L: USD -20.4M, flowing through COGS to gross profit to EBITDA. Total portfolio unrealised P&L (FX + commodity): USD -67.4M.

The 3-month maturity cluster across FX003, COM001, and COM002 represents a near-term roll concentration risk. If these positions are rolled at less favourable rates, the unrealised loss position may widen.

Hedge ratio deterioration across USD_CNY and USD_EUR — both below the 45% breach floor — warrants immediate CFO-approved hedge ratio remediation proposals. The USD_CNY position additionally carries S-01 geopolitical currency pair exposure; see interconnection flags.

---

### F-02: Revenue Concentration & Credit Quality

**Status: BREACH (bad_debt) / AMBER (concentration) | Trend: DETERIORATING**

**Top Customer Concentration:** 22.1% (Enterprise Tier1 segment), against an amber threshold of 20% and breach threshold of 25%. Status: AMBER. No directional data from prior period is available in the current dataset to establish a QoQ trend; however, the value exceeds the amber floor.

**Bad Debt Provision Rate:** 1.44% of total receivables, against an amber threshold of 0.40%, a breach threshold of 0.55%, and the COV006 covenant threshold of 0.80%. Status: BREACH — the provision rate is 2.6× the breach threshold and 1.8× the covenant threshold. This is the most severe KRI breach in the financial domain.

Segment-level provision analysis:
- Enterprise Tier1: USD 12.4M provision. Lowest relative provision rate in the portfolio given USD 3,070M total receivables in segment. 90d overdue: USD 8M.
- Enterprise Tier2: USD 28.6M provision. 90d overdue: USD 18M — second highest in absolute terms.
- SMB Tier3: USD 32.1M provision — highest absolute provision. 90d overdue: USD 22M — highest absolute 90d overdue balance. This segment is the primary driver of provision rate elevation.
- Government Tier1: USD 2.8M provision. Zero 90d overdue balance — lowest credit risk segment.
- Consumer Tier4: USD 18.4M provision. 90d overdue: USD 12M. Highest overdue rate relative to current receivables (USD 420M current).

Total bad debt provision (aggregate across all segments, for COV006 cure computation): USD 94.3M. Total 90d overdue: USD 60M.

The SMB Tier3 and Consumer Tier4 segments collectively account for the majority of 90d overdue balances and absolute provision values. Any cure strategy must prioritise write-off or accelerated collection actions in these segments.

---

### F-03: Liquidity & Refinancing

**Status: BREACH (COV006) / AMBER (COV001, COV004) / OK (COV002, COV003, COV005) | Trend: DETERIORATING**

#### COV006 — CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH (Trade-Finance Facility)
Bad_debt_provision_pct: 1.44% vs threshold 0.80%. Headroom: -0.64%. This breach deepened QoQ from -0.30% headroom at the 2026-03-31 test to -0.64% at the current 2026-06-30 test. Cure deadline: **2026-06-12**.

**MANDATORY BOARD AND LENDER ACTION REQUIREMENTS:**
(a) BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED — lender notification is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms.
(b) Written board authorisation is required before the Group Treasurer makes any contact with lenders.
(c) Hard deadline: board resolution must be achieved by **2026-06-12**.
(d) Named joint owners: **Group Treasurer AND CFO** — CFO co-signature is required per facility terms.
(e) Cross-default risk: failure to meet the lender notification obligation before the 2026-06-30 test date creates material cross-default risk across RCF-USD2B and Term-Loan-A facilities.

Cure scenarios:
- Full cure (USD 94.3M write-off): P&L impact USD 94.3M, reducing EBITDA from USD 2.28B to approximately USD 2.186B. COV001 EBITDA headroom falls from USD 152M to approximately USD 58M.
- Partial cure (USD 50M write-off): EBITDA headroom falls from USD 152M to approximately USD 102M.
- No cure / waiver obtained: facility technical default on Trade-Finance; cross-default risk materialises.

#### COV001 — Net Debt / EBITDA (RCF-USD2B)
Current ratio: 2.8×. Threshold: 3.0×. Headroom in ratio units: 0.2×. **EBITDA headroom in dollar terms: USD 152M** (derived as: Net Debt = 2.8× USD 2.28B = USD 6.384B; COV001 EBITDA floor = USD 6.384B / 3.0 = USD 2.128B; headroom = USD 2.28B − USD 2.128B = USD 152M). QoQ deterioration: ratio moved from 2.6 (2026-03-31) to 2.8 (2026-06-30). No active deleveraging programme is referenced in the provided data. Status: AMBER, trend: DETERIORATING.

#### COV002 — Interest Coverage (Term-Loan-A)
Current ratio: 4.2×. Threshold: 3.5×. Headroom: 0.7×. QoQ deterioration: ratio declined from 4.8 (2026-03-31) to 4.2 (2026-06-30). Status: OK, but trajectory is negative.

#### COV003 — Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B)
Current: USD 1.1B. Threshold: USD 0.8B. Headroom: USD 0.3B. The USD 0.9B escalation trigger provides a USD 200M buffer at current levels. QoQ deterioration: headroom fell from USD 0.4B (2026-03-31) to USD 0.3B (2026-06-30). Status: OK, but approaching amber zone. If COV006 cross-default rights are exercised by lenders, RCF drawdown capacity may be constrained, compressing effective liquidity headroom below reported levels.

#### COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027)
Current: 8 months. Threshold: 12 months. Headroom: 0 months (below threshold). Escalation trigger: 6 months — current runway provides 2-month buffer above escalation. QoQ deterioration: runway declined from 11 months (2026-03-31) to 8 months (2026-12-01 next test). The active COV006 breach may constrain refinancing access and pricing for Bond-2027 refinancing. Status: AMBER, trend: DETERIORATING.

#### COV005 — EBITDA (All-Facilities)
Current: USD 2.28B. Threshold: USD 1.80B. Headroom: USD 0.48B on standalone EBITDA covenant basis. However, effective headroom is governed by COV001 at USD 152M, making COV005 a secondary constraint. QoQ deterioration: EBITDA declined USD 0.23B from USD 2.51B.

**Combined stress scenario:** If FX unrealised P&L (USD -47.0M), commodity unrealised P&L (USD -20.4M), and full COV006 cure write-off (USD -94.3M) all flow through EBITDA simultaneously, total EBITDA erosion is USD 161.7M — exceeding the USD 152M COV001 headroom by USD 9.7M and placing COV001 in technical breach. This scenario must be assessed by Treasury and CFO as an acute downside case.

---

### F-04: Financial Audit & Reporting

**Status: OK | Trend: FLAT**

AUD003 Internal-SOX (Financial-Controls) is completed with 1 open high-severity finding and 0 critical findings. No material weakness has been identified. The amber threshold is 2 high findings; the breach threshold is 5. Current count of 1 is below the amber threshold. Status: OK.

The 1 open high finding does not constitute a material weakness under the provided data. However, its content is not specified in the audit log extract provided; if it relates to bad debt provisioning methodology or FX hedge accounting, it would have direct cross-domain implications for F-02 and F-01 respectively. This data gap is noted — confidence on F-04 cross-domain implications is low absent finding detail.

---

### Summary of Escalation Items
1. **COV006 ACTIVE BREACH** — immediate board and lender action required. Cure/waiver deadline 2026-06-12. Cross-default risk to RCF-USD2B and Term-Loan-A if notification obligation not met. Joint ownership: Group Treasurer + CFO.
2. **F-01 Unhedged FX Exposure** — USD 4,080M exceeds amber threshold of USD 4,000M. USD_CNY (43.8% hedge ratio) and USD_EUR (42.9% hedge ratio) both below the 45% breach floor; CFO-approved hedge ratio remediation required.
3. **Combined EBITDA stress** — concurrent realisation of FX/commodity mark-to-market losses and full COV006 cure write-off yields USD 161.7M EBITDA erosion against USD 152M COV001 headroom, implying COV001 breach in the acute downside scenario.

board_summary:
RISK POSTURE

The portfolio carries 17 KRI breaches and 18 amber warnings across all four risk domains; concurrent breach of four domains activates the systemic risk flag, requiring board-level attention.

STRATEGIC: The strategic domain records one breach and two ambers, accountable to the Chief Executive Officer. S-01 has breached on geopolitical concentration with five signals detected; S-02 is amber on competitive AI displacement; S-03 holds within bounds on M&A integration. The active S-01+F-01 compound scenario links geopolitical signals to elevated FX exposure, warranting immediate review against current hedging positions. The S-03+F-02 compound scenario flags three active deals — CloudScale Systems, NeuralEdge AI, and DataVault Security — which may shift customer revenue concentration materially.

OPERATIONAL: The operational domain is the most acute exposure, recording ten breaches and seven ambers, accountable across sub-domains to the COO, CISO, and CHRO. Quanta [SUP005] and TSMC [SUP001] are single-source suppliers with zero percent cyber resilience assessment coverage, providing no cyber posture visibility into partners handling USD 5.8 billion of spend. A cyber incident at either supplier would simultaneously trigger O-01 continuity failure and O-02 third-party breach scenarios. Compounding this, TSMC carries an 18-week lead time and Quanta a 3.9-week inventory buffer; with seven critical Engineering roles open beyond 60 days and three R&D flight risks, the organisation cannot execute accelerated dual-sourcing within the five-month recovery window. IT-Operations attrition of 11.8 percent and understaffed security operations are directly consistent with patch compliance at 71 percent, mean time to detect at 11 days, and privileged access unreviewed for 45 days. NOTE: O-03 field_failure_rate_pct is PROVISIONAL — UNDER RECONCILIATION (QMS-SAP: 0.048% authoritative per CRO decision; warranty system: 1.9% — 40x discrepancy; board materials must label this KRI as PROVISIONAL until reconciled by 2026-06-20). Separately, Quanta's rejection rate of 1.2 percent — the highest among single-source suppliers against an amber portfolio rate of 0.68 percent — combined with a financial distress score of 61 risks simultaneous yield degradation and warranty reserve increases, amplifying EBITDA impact.

FINANCIAL: [P(ANY COVENANT BREACH) = 100% CURRENT — COV006 CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH as of 2026-05-01. The 49.7% forward probability below refers to ADDITIONAL COV001 breach only.] The financial domain records four breaches and seven ambers, accountable to the Chief Financial Officer. The FX-only portfolio carries aggregate unhedged exposure of USD 4,080 million, above the USD 4,000 million amber threshold; USD/CNY is the largest single unhedged exposure at USD 1,800 million with a hedge ratio of 43.8 percent — below the 45 percent breach floor — counterparty JPMorgan, and subject to the S-01 geopolitical flag. USD/EUR is the second-largest unhedged position at USD 1,600 million, hedge ratio 42.9 percent, counterparty Deutsche Bank, also below the breach floor. FX positions unrealised loss of USD 26.6 million and commodity unrealised loss of USD 20.4 million represent total combined mark-to-market stress of USD 47.0 million (CORRECTED 2026-06-09: prior USD 67.4 million double-counted commodity). If realised or provisioned alongside a full COV006 cure write-off of USD 94.3 million, total EBITDA erosion would reach USD 141.3 million (cure USD 94.3M + combined MTM USD 47.0M) — leaving USD 10.7 million residual COV001 headroom (no deterministic breach under combined scenario; forward P(additional COV001 breach at 2026-06-30) = 49.7% provisional, conditional on COV006 active breach); this combined scenario must be explicitly stress-tested. The bad debt provision breach at 1.44 percent is the direct cause of the active COV006 breach; a full write-off reduces EBITDA from USD 2.28 billion to approximately USD 2.186 billion, compressing COV001 headroom from USD 152 million to USD 58 million. SMB Tier-3 and Enterprise Tier-2 segments carry the highest 90-day overdue balances of USD 22 million and USD 18 million respectively, concentrating credit risk outside Tier-1. Bond-2027 maturity runway of eight months sits below the 12-month covenant threshold and only two months above the six-month escalation trigger; refinancing access may be constrained by the active COV006 breach disclosure. If lenders exercise cross-default rights, RCF-USD 2 billion and Term-Loan-A facilities could be affected, further compressing liquidity headroom currently at USD 1.1 billion — USD 200 million above the USD 0.9 billion escalation trigger.

COMPLIANCE: The compliance domain records two breaches and two ambers, accountable to the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel. C-02 is in breach on EU AI Act conformity assessment coverage, and C-03 is in breach on anti-bribery third-party coverage under FCPA and the UK Bribery Act; C-01 carries amber conditions on export screening coverage. Three denied-party matches remain pending resolution in the Trade Compliance System; audit AUD001 on Tier-1 supplier ABAC has completed with two high findings. If any Tier-1 supplier implicated in those high ABAC findings also appears in the pending denied-party queue, a compound C-01/C-03 exposure exists; the CCO must cross-reference denied-party match identities against ABAC findings immediately. Additionally, DORA — effective 17 January 2025 — and NIS2 — effective 18 October 2024 — are mapped to C-01 but carry untested incident response plan gaps with direct implications for systems processing personal data, creating a potential C-02 exposure; the Chief Privacy Officer and Operational Agent responsible for O-02 must be notified, and the Chief Risk Officer is flagged to adjudicate the regulatory mapping anomaly.

KEY RISK DRIVERS

The most consequential combination of findings this period is the convergence of supply chain fragility, degraded cyber defences, and active covenant stress — three conditions that individually would each require urgent board attention but together create a scenario in which a single triggering event could cascade across all three simultaneously. The supply chain position is structurally exposed: 62.6% of spend is sourced from a single supplier against a 50% threshold, geographic concentration has breached its 75% limit at 75.3%, inventory cover has fallen below the minimum four-week threshold to 3.9 weeks, and not one supplier has been assessed for cyber resilience against a 50% target. The critical single-source supplier Quanta carries a financial health score below the distress threshold, an on-time delivery rate of 88.7%, and the highest rejection rate in the portfolio — making it the most likely near-term failure point in a supply base that already has no redundancy. If a disruption materialises, the 18-week lead time for TSMC means there is no operationally meaningful short-term substitute, and the five-month recovery window modelled by the supply chain team would require significant engineering and procurement capacity that the organisation currently does not have: seven critical engineering roles have been open for more than 60 days, three R&D staff are flagged as flight risks, and the average time to fill a critical engineering role is 68 days. The cyber domain compounds this directly — mean time to detect a security incident is 11 days against a 10-day threshold, patch compliance is 71% against an 80% minimum, multi-factor authentication covers only 87% of systems against a 90% requirement, and privileged access accounts have gone unreviewed for 45 days. These are not independent weaknesses; the operational agent explicitly links them to IT-Operations capacity constraints arising from the same talent shortage driving the engineering gap. Overlaid on this operational picture is an active covenant breach: the bad debt provision rate of 1.44% is 2.6 times the breach threshold and has breached the Trade Finance facility covenant with a cure deadline that requires board-authorised lender notification this week. The USD 152 million of headroom under the primary EBITDA covenant is materially at risk — a full cure write-off alone consumes USD 94.3 million of that headroom, and if the combined USD 47.0 million FX and commodity mark-to-market losses (FX positions USD 26.6 million + commodity USD 20.4 million) were simultaneously required to flow through EBITDA, total EBITDA erosion would reach USD 141.3 million against COV001 headroom of USD 152 million — leaving USD 10.7 million residual headroom with no deterministic breach under the combined scenario. NOTE: P(ANY covenant breach) = 100% CURRENT because COV006 is in confirmed active breach. The board's immediate decisions on lender notification, supply chain structural intervention, and cyber remediation resourcing are therefore not sequential — they are parallel and time-critical.

CROSS-DOMAIN CONNECTIONS

The most material cross-domain interaction this period runs from talent through cyber into supply chain, and from there into the financial covenant position. The understaffing of IT-Operations — evidenced by seven critical roles open beyond 60 days and a tech attrition rate of 14.2% against a 12% amber threshold — is the proximate cause of the degraded cyber control environment: an organisation with full security operations staffing does not accumulate 45 days of unreviewed privileged access, allow patch compliance to fall to 71%, or permit mean detection time to extend to 11 days. This matters beyond IT because the organisation's two most critical single-source suppliers — handling USD 5.8 billion of spend combined — have had zero cyber resilience assessments completed, meaning a cyber incident originating at either supplier would simultaneously trigger a supply continuity failure with no early warning. That supply failure would arrive on a balance sheet already under covenant stress: the five-month recovery window modelled for a major supply disruption would involve premium spot-market sourcing, potential line stoppages, and contractual penalties, all of which would flow through EBITDA at a time when COV001 headroom stands at only USD 152 million before the bad debt cure and mark-to-market adjustments described above. A second interaction connects the geopolitical environment to the FX hedge book: three of the five active geopolitical signals — US Entity List expansion, Taiwan Strait military exercises, and the EU reciprocal tariff review on Chinese electronics — bear directly on the USD/CNY and USD/EUR positions, which at hedge ratios of 43.8% and 42.9% respectively are both below the 45% individual pair minimum, leaving USD 1.8 billion and USD 1.6 billion respectively exposed to exactly the currency volatility that escalating US-China technology decoupling and regional trade retaliation would produce. A third connection links the compliance domain to supply chain: the three pending denied-party matches in the trade compliance system have not yet been cross-referenced against the two high findings from the completed Tier 1 anti-bribery and corruption audit — if any Tier 1 supplier appearing in both is also one of the single-source suppliers, the organisation would face simultaneous sanctions, ABAC, and supply continuity exposure from a single counterparty. Finally, the cyber resilience agent's identification of hyperscaler infrastructure fragility as a near-term emerging risk amplifies the existing O-02 position: if the organisation's digital stack is disrupted by an external cloud outage at the same time as internal cyber controls are degraded and supply chain inventory cover is below minimum, the compounding BCM scenario has no current tested response plan — the compliance agent flags that DORA and NIS2 incident response plans remain untested despite both obligations having been live since 2024 and 2025 respectively.

QUARTER-ON-QUARTER MOVEMENT

Aggregate residual risk has risen materially from the prior period. Fifteen of the twenty KRIs tracked across the comparison period show a deteriorating trend direction, against three improving and two stable. The dominant cause is inherent risk growth — the underlying threat environment has expanded rather than controls simply failing to keep pace. Four KRIs moved into breach status this period that were previously within threshold or amber: mean time to detect crossed from amber to breach as detection time extended from 9 to 11 days; critical open roles crossed from amber to breach as the count rose from five to seven; inventory cover crossed from amber to breach as weeks of cover fell from 4.3 to 3.9; and geopolitical signal count crossed from amber to breach as active signals increased from two to five. Within continuing breaches, the bad debt provision rate rose from 1.24% to 1.44%, deepening the covenant breach on the Trade Finance facility. The hedge ratio fell from 50.3% to 46.0%, narrowing the buffer above the 45% floor. Patch compliance deteriorated further from 78% to 71% despite remaining a breach throughout. Recall readiness moved from acceptable to amber, declining from 82% to 72%. Three KRIs improved — AI audit coverage, third-party anti-bribery coverage, and synergy delivery — but none cleared their breach thresholds on the compliance measures, meaning the improving trend has not yet produced remediation. The single KRI that, if reversed, would most reduce aggregate residual risk is mean time to detect: restoring detection capability to within the 10-day threshold is the board's primary lever this period, because it directly reduces the window during which a threat actor can operate undetected across a control environment that is simultaneously weakened by low patch compliance, unreviewed privileged access, and incomplete MFA coverage.

RISK COMMITTEE RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
  • Direct the CFO to formally notify lenders of the active COV006 breach this week and to present to the next Committee meeting a written stress test showing the combined EBITDA impact of the $94.3M bad debt cure write-off, $47.0M combined portfolio MTM losses (FX positions $26.6M + commodity $20.4M) against the COV001 headroom of $152M — combined scenario total erosion $141.3M leaving $10.7M residual COV001 headroom (no deterministic breach; forward P(additional COV001 breach) = 49.7% provisional). NOTE: P(any covenant breach) = 100% current — COV006 is already in confirmed breach requiring lender notification regardless of COV001 headroom.
  • Direct the CISO to deliver within 30 days a time-bound remediation plan restoring patch compliance from 71% to the 80% threshold, MTTD from 11 days to the 10-day threshold, MFA coverage from 87% to the 90% threshold, and clearing the 45-day privileged access review backlog; require the CISO to report fortnightly to the CRO until all four metrics are within threshold, given that the IT recovery time objective is simultaneously at its 6-hour limit with no buffer.
  • Direct the COO and VP Supply Chain to initiate formal dual-sourcing qualification for the suppliers driving the 62.6% single-source concentration breach, and to implement an emergency inventory buffer plan to restore cover above 4 weeks; require the COO to commission cyber resilience assessments for 100% of critical single-source suppliers — currently at 0% against a 50% threshold — and to report progress at each Committee meeting, noting that a disruption at either assessed supplier involves an 18-week lead-time recovery window the organisation currently cannot staff.
  • Require the CHRO to present at the next Committee meeting a plan to resolve the 7 critical open roles exceeding 60 days, with particular focus on engineering and IT security functions; the Committee notes that understaffing in these functions is a direct contributing factor to the degraded cyber posture (patch compliance 71%, MTTD 11 days) and would prevent execution of a supplier dual-sourcing programme within the 5-month recovery window if a supply disruption occurs simultaneously.
  • Direct the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel to cross-reference the three pending denied-party matches against the two high findings from the completed third-party anti-bribery and corruption audit immediately and to report conclusions to the Committee within 14 days; require the CCO to present a remediation plan to lift third-party anti-bribery and corruption coverage from 89% to the 95% threshold and AI audit coverage from 78% to the 90% threshold, and to confirm whether any regulatory notification obligations are triggered by the active gaps.

cov001_board_narrative.text: IMPORTANT — COV001 AMBER RATIO VS FORWARD BREACH PROBABILITY: The Net Debt to EBITDA ratio currently reads 2.8× (amber — below the 3.0× breach ceiling). This ratio reflects the position as at the Q2 reporting date and is correct. However, the EBITDA Stress Monte Carlo separately estimates a 69.4% probability that this ratio will exceed 3.0× by the 2026-06-30 test date — a breach-status forward indicator. These two signals are not contradictory: the ratio measures where the organisation is today; the Monte Carlo measures where it is likely to be in 23 days under plausible EBITDA scenarios. Both signals must be read together. The amber ratio does NOT indicate the covenant position is under control — the forward probability of 69.4% means there is a greater-than-two-thirds chance of a COV001 breach at the next test date. The board should treat this as a breach-trajectory situation requiring the same urgency as an active breach. A deterministic scenario in which the COV006 provision charge of approximately USD 365M is recognised in full would reduce EBITDA to approximately USD 1,915M — below the COV001 implied floor of USD 2,128M — giving P(breach) = 100% at test date.
